scenario: Vanta Marketplace introduces an AI agent step into a customer-support process with 2500 events per day in Camunda 8.8. The agent must call a model-defined dynamic set of tools where each tool is one inner activity in a subprocess, and the model decides at runtime which tools to invoke and in which order.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 BPMN subprocess type backs this AI agent pattern with model-selected tool invocations?

options.text: An event subprocess holds the tools; the AI agent step triggers the event subprocess's start event once per model-selected tool invocation
An ad-hoc subprocess holds the tools; the AI agent step uses the FEEL completion condition to end the loop once the model finishes
An embedded subprocess holds the tools in a fixed sequence flow; the AI agent step skips the unselected tools through conditional sequence flows
A call activity holds the tools; the AI agent step invokes one of the deployed tool processes per iteration through the model decision

b.text: Correct. The AI agent pattern in Camunda 8.8 uses an ad-hoc subprocess whose inner activities are tools the model can choose to run in any order, with a completion condition driving the loop end.

d.text: Incorrect. Call activities invoke a single deployed process per activation; they do not model a model-selected set of inner tools.

a.text: Incorrect. Event subprocesses are triggered by events inside a parent scope, not by a model's tool-selection loop.

c.text: Incorrect. Embedded subprocesses follow fixed sequence flows; they cannot model an unordered tool set chosen at runtime by a model.

explanation: Camunda 8.8's AI agent pattern is built on the ad-hoc subprocess; inner activities are tools, the model selects them dynamically, and the completion condition drives the loop end.

scenario: Lattice Bank picks one of 5 child process definitions at runtime based on the variable productType (loan, mortgage, card, deposit, fx) across 11000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The call activity must resolve the target processId dynamically per instance.
question: How is the target processId configured on a call activity for runtime selection of the child definition?

options.text: Set the processId to *.process on the call activity; the engine performs a wildcard lookup using the productType process variable at activation
Set the processId on the call activity to one of the 5 literal ids and route to alternatives through a downstream exclusive gateway
Set the implementation type to dynamic-call on the call activity; the engine reads the target id from a custom header named processId
Set a FEEL processId expression on the call activity like =productType + "-process"; the engine resolves the target definition at activation

d.text: Correct. Call activities accept a FEEL processId expression in Camunda 8; the engine resolves the target definition id at activation time.

b.text: Incorrect. Static literals plus exclusive gateways add ceremony; the documented mechanism is a FEEL expression on the call activity itself.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no wildcard processId resolution in Camunda 8; FEEL expressions are the documented mechanism.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no dynamic-call implementation type; the FEEL processId expression on the call activity is the supported control.

explanation: Call activities accept a FEEL processId expression that the engine evaluates at activation to resolve the target child process definition.

scenario: Meridian Logistics calls a child process from 7000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. The team wants every variable created inside the child process to flow up into the parent scope on completion, without authoring an output mapping for each individual variable on the call activity element.
question: Which call activity property propagates every child variable to the parent scope on completion in Camunda 8?

options.text: Set the propagate-mode header to all on the call activity; the engine reads the custom header and propagates every child variable
Set the implementation type to merge-child on the call activity; the engine writes every child variable into the parent scope on completion
Set inputCollection to all on the call activity; the engine treats the option as a wildcard for every variable in the child scope
Enable propagateAllChildVariables on the call activity; every variable from the completed child instance is written to the parent scope

d.text: Correct. Camunda 8 call activities support propagateAllChildVariables to copy every child variable to the parent on completion without per-variable output mappings.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no merge-child implementation type; the documented mechanism is the propagateAllChildVariables boolean property.

a.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static metadata on the activity, not a propagation switch for child variables.

c.text: Incorrect. inputCollection belongs to multi-instance markers and applies to per-iteration inputs, not to call activity variable propagation.

explanation: Call activities expose propagateAllChildVariables to copy every variable from the completed child instance back to the parent scope automatically.

scenario: Forge Bank processes 14000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The job worker for service tasks needs static configuration (an endpoint URL and a region code) that varies by task instance in the BPMN but never changes across process instances and is not stored as a process variable.
question: Which Camunda 8 service task field carries static per-element configuration to the job worker?

options.text: Custom headers on the service task; the broker copies them onto the activated job and the worker reads them from the job metadata
Documentation on the service task; the broker passes the documentation string as a header and the worker parses key-value pairs from it
Input mappings on the service task; the broker evaluates them once per process deployment and the worker reads them from the job variables
Process variables defined at deployment; the broker treats them as immutable header data and the worker reads them as task headers

a.text: Correct. Custom headers on a service task are static per-element metadata that the broker copies onto the activated job for the worker to read.

c.text: Incorrect. Input mappings produce variables per activation against process scope, not per-element static metadata.

d.text: Incorrect. Process variables flow with the instance and are mutable; they are not the documented vehicle for static per-element configuration.

b.text: Incorrect. Documentation is human-readable text and is not parsed for configuration in Camunda 8.

explanation: Custom headers attach static metadata per service task element; the broker copies them onto the activated job so the worker can use them as configuration.

scenario: Umbra Insurance attaches scanned PDF documents to 4000 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. The team must pass the document content across multiple service tasks without inflating process variables, using the platform's built-in document handling introduced for stream-oriented uploads of records of variable size.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 mechanism passes a binary document through a process by reference rather than embedding bytes in process variables?

options.text: Documents are stored in a per-instance Elasticsearch index; workers query the index using the process instance key as the lookup key
Documents are passed as custom headers on every service task; the broker copies header bytes onto each job activation without overhead
Camunda 8.8 documents are stored in the document store; the process variable holds a reference and workers fetch the bytes by document id
Documents are base64-encoded inside process variables; the broker compresses them on write so they remain small in the broker state machine

c.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 introduced first-class document handling: documents live in the document store and process variables carry references for workers to dereference.

d.text: Incorrect. Base64 in process variables is the legacy anti-pattern that document handling replaces; the broker does not auto-compress.

a.text: Incorrect. Documents are not stored in the runtime Operate index; a dedicated document store is the documented mechanism.

b.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static text metadata; they are not the document-passing mechanism in Camunda 8.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 documents live in the document store; process variables carry a reference object that workers and connectors use to read or write document bytes.

scenario: Junction Retail handles 7500 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on an end error event must compute the BPMN error code from a runtime variable (e.g., "INVENTORY_" + skuPrefix) so a matching boundary error event with the same dynamic code catches the error.
question: How is a dynamic FEEL-driven BPMN error code authored on the throw error end event?

options.text: Set the error code on the end error event to a JUEL expression referencing skuPrefix; Camunda 8 supports JUEL expressions for error codes on the end error event
Set the error code on the end error event to the literal string INVENTORY_*; the engine substitutes the wildcard from the process variable
Set the error code expression on the end error event to a FEEL expression like ="INVENTORY_" + skuPrefix; the engine evaluates it at throw time
Set the error code on the end error event to %skuPrefix%; the engine performs environment-style variable expansion at throw time

c.text: Correct. Camunda 8 error events accept a FEEL error code expression that the engine evaluates at throw time to produce the runtime error code.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no wildcard substitution in BPMN error codes; the documented mechanism is a FEEL expression.

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 uses FEEL, not JUEL, for runtime expressions on BPMN elements.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no percent-sign expansion in Camunda 8 error codes; the documented expression language is FEEL.

explanation: BPMN error events in Camunda 8 accept a FEEL error code expression that is evaluated at throw time to produce the runtime error code for matching boundary handlers.

scenario: Ridge Logistics handles 6000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping on a service task must extract two specific fields (id and weightKg) from a complex JSON variable named shipment and write them as separate top-level process variables on completion.
question: Which FEEL expression on the output mapping yields a context (map) containing just those two fields?

options.text: Use a FEEL XPath expression like /shipment/[id|weightKg] as the source expression on the output mapping for the service task
Use a FEEL projection like { id: shipment.id, weightKg: shipment.weightKg } as the source expression on the output mapping
Use a FEEL list projection like shipment[id, weightKg] as the source expression on the output mapping; FEEL pick converts lists to contexts
Use a FEEL spread expression like { ...shipment[id, weightKg] } as the source expression on the output mapping for the service task

b.text: Correct. FEEL supports inline context construction with key:value pairs, which is the documented way to project two named fields from a larger object.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not have a spread or rest operator; explicit key:value pairs are the documented projection mechanism.

c.text: Incorrect. Bracket syntax on a context is a single key access, not a multi-key projection; FEEL has no built-in pick on contexts.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not use XPath; it uses a context and list syntax described in the FEEL language guide.

explanation: FEEL supports inline context construction with key:value pairs; this is the documented way to project specific fields from a larger object into a result context.

scenario: Xander Logistics filters 7000 records per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping on a service task must produce the sublist of lineItems whose priceEur is at least 100, given a process variable lineItems that is a list of contexts with a priceEur field.
question: Which FEEL expression filters the list of contexts in Camunda 8?

options.text: lineItems.where(priceEur >= 100) uses the FEEL dot-where method on every list in Camunda 8 FEEL with full predicate access
lineItems[item.priceEur >= 100] uses the FEEL list filter syntax with item referring to the current element of the list
lineItems.filter(priceEur >= 100) uses the FEEL dot-filter method on every list in Camunda 8 FEEL with full predicate access
filter(lineItems, item.priceEur >= 100) uses a built-in higher-order FEEL function on the list with a lambda predicate

b.text: Correct. FEEL list filter syntax is list[predicate]; the variable item refers to the current element when iterating contexts.

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda FEEL does not include a top-level filter() higher-order function; bracket-predicate is the documented form.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not a method-chained language; .where is not a documented operator on lists.

c.text: Incorrect. Same as above; .filter is not a documented FEEL operator on lists in Camunda 8.

explanation: FEEL filters lists with bracket-predicate syntax (list[predicate]); inside the predicate, item refers to the current element.

scenario: Yarrow Bank processes 12000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping must compute the next-business-day date by adding 1 day to today and writing the result as the dueDate process variable for downstream timer events to use.
question: Which FEEL expression adds 1 day to the current date in Camunda 8 FEEL?

options.text: date.add(today(), 1) calls a built-in FEEL date helper that takes a date and a day count and returns a new date value
today() + 1 uses FEEL number coercion to add 1 day to the current date and return a date value as the result
today() + duration("P1D") uses the FEEL date function and ISO 8601 duration to add 1 day to the current date
today() + P1D treats P1D as an unquoted FEEL identifier referring to a built-in 1-day constant for date arithmetic

c.text: Correct. FEEL adds durations to dates with the date(date) + duration(ISO8601) form; today() returns a date and duration("P1D") is one day.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not coerce numbers to durations; adding a bare 1 to a date is a type error.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no date.add helper; the standard arithmetic operator handles the case via durations.

d.text: Incorrect. P1D is not a FEEL identifier; durations are created with the duration(string) function.

explanation: FEEL date arithmetic uses date + duration(...) with ISO 8601 duration strings; today() + duration("P1D") yields tomorrow's date.

scenario: Wren Bank processes 8000 invoices per day in Camunda 8.8 with Intelligent Document Processing. The team must extract a configured set of fields (invoiceNumber, totalEur, dueDate) from each uploaded PDF document inside the BPMN flow and write the extracted values back to process variables for downstream business rule evaluation.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 BPMN element invokes the IDP extraction step and persists the extracted fields as process variables?

options.text: A business rule task with IDP as the decision engine type; the engine runs OCR plus DMN in one call against the uploaded document
A user task with IDP enabled on the form; Tasklist runs the extraction client-side and writes the resulting values to process variables
A receive task subscribed to a message named idp-extract; the broker correlates extraction results from the document store automatically
An IDP application invoked from a service task or connector; the configured field set is extracted and written back as named process variables

d.text: Correct. IDP is invoked from a service task or a dedicated connector; the documented field set is extracted and written back as process variables.

b.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not run IDP client-side; extraction is a backend service or connector invocation in Camunda 8.8.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no implicit idp-extract message subscription; IDP is invoked synchronously from a task element.

a.text: Incorrect. Business rule tasks evaluate DMN; IDP is a separate component invoked from a service task or connector, not a DMN engine type.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 IDP runs as an application invoked from a service task or connector; the extracted field set is written back as process variables for downstream steps.

scenario: Atlas Insurance processes 9500 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. A service task expects a job variable named amountCents while the process variable is named claimAmountCents. The team must rename the variable for the job worker only, without changing the process-scoped variable upstream.
question: Which Camunda 8 task configuration renames the variable on the job activation without touching the process variable?

options.text: Rename the upstream process variable globally; the job worker SDK accepts only canonical variable names and rejects mappings at activation
Configure a custom header amountCents=claimAmountCents on the service task; the engine swaps the variable name at job activation time
Add an output mapping with source claimAmountCents and target amountCents on the service task; the job sees the renamed variable
Add an input mapping with source claimAmountCents and target amountCents on the service task; the job sees the renamed variable

d.text: Correct. Input mappings on a task evaluate FEEL expressions before activation and expose them under the configured target names on the job scope.

c.text: Incorrect. Output mappings transform variables that flow from the task back to the process scope, not the other direction at activation.

a.text: Incorrect. Renaming process variables affects every consumer; input mappings are the documented per-task rename mechanism.

b.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static metadata for the worker; they do not rename variables at activation in the engine.

explanation: Input mappings rename or compute variables for a task at activation; the source FEEL expression evaluates against process scope and the result is bound to the target name on the task scope.

scenario: Echo Telco runs 22000 requests per day in Camunda 8.8 against a fragile downstream API. The architect must configure the service task so transient failures are retried 5 times by the engine before an incident is raised on the failed job by the broker.
question: Which Camunda 8 property controls how many times the engine retries a job before raising an incident?

options.text: Set the timeout property on the service task to 5 seconds; the broker retries the job every 5 seconds until 0 are left
Set the assignee property on the service task to 5; the broker activates the job 5 times in parallel for redundancy purposes
Set the implementation type to retry-on-failure with a value of 5; the broker reads the type discriminator and retries that many times
Set the retries property on the service task to 5; the broker decrements retries on failure and raises an incident when it reaches 0

d.text: Correct. The retries property on a service task seeds the job's retries counter; the broker raises an incident when retries reach 0.

a.text: Incorrect. Timeout configures how long an activation lock is held by the worker, not the number of retries on failure.

b.text: Incorrect. Assignee is a user task property; it does not apply to service tasks and does not control retry counts.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no retry-on-failure implementation type; the retries property is the documented control.

explanation: The retries property on a service task initialises the job's retries counter; the broker raises an incident when the counter reaches 0 after worker-reported failures.

scenario: Tessera Telco runs 30000 jobs per hour in Camunda 8.8. The job worker's average completion time spikes during peak hours, and the architect must set the activation timeout so a worker has 120 seconds to complete a job before the broker re-activates the job for another worker.
question: Which property sets the activation lock duration on a job activation in Camunda 8?

options.text: Set the assignee property to 120s on the user task; the broker reuses the assignee field as an activation timeout for adjacent service tasks
Set the activation timeout on the worker (or the request) to 120 seconds; the broker holds the job locked for the worker until the timeout elapses
Set the custom header timeout=120 on the service task; the broker reads the header and treats the value as the activation timeout in seconds
Set the retries property on the service task to 120; the broker uses the retries field as a per-second timeout when the worker is connected

b.text: Correct. Job activation specifies a lock timeout per call; the broker re-activates the job for another worker if the timeout elapses before completion.

d.text: Incorrect. Retries is a separate counter for failure recovery, not a per-activation timeout.

a.text: Incorrect. Assignee is a user task property and does not control job activation timeouts for service tasks.

c.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static metadata; the timeout is a property of the activation request, not of the service task element.

explanation: Job activation in Camunda 8 takes a timeout per call; the broker re-activates the job if the worker does not complete it within that lock duration.

scenario: Delta Insurance handles 12000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A service task must dispatch work to the team's Java job worker which subscribes to the type credit-check. The architect must configure the service task so the broker activates a job with the matching type for that worker.
question: Which property on the service task tells the broker which job worker is supposed to activate the job?

options.text: Set the job type property on the service task to credit-check; the broker creates a job of that type for matching workers to activate
Set the documentation property to credit-check; the broker reads the documentation field to route jobs to workers across the cluster
Set the activity id property to credit-check; the broker matches the activity id against the worker's configured subscription string
Set the implementation property to the fully qualified class name of the Java job worker; the broker injects an instance at activation time

a.text: Correct. Service tasks in Camunda 8 declare a job type; the broker creates a job of that type and any worker subscribed to it can activate it.

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 does not invoke Java classes by name; the worker subscribes to a job type and is decoupled from the broker.

c.text: Incorrect. Activity id is the BPMN element id and is not used for routing jobs to workers; the routing key is the job type.

b.text: Incorrect. Documentation is human-readable metadata and is not consulted by the broker for job routing.

explanation: The job type property on a service task is the routing key the broker uses to match a job to a job worker that subscribes to that type.

scenario: Iris Bank correlates 9000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 by orderId between an external payment system and running process instances. The team must configure the message intermediate catch event so each published message reaches exactly the one instance whose orderId matches the message payload.
question: Which message event configuration ensures point-to-point correlation by the orderId process variable?

options.text: Set the message subscription scope to per-tenant on the message reference; the broker uses the tenant id implicitly for cross-instance correlation
Set a correlation key expression like =orderId on the message reference; the broker uses the value to locate the unique waiting instance
Set the message name to orderId on the message reference; the broker matches messages to waiting instances by the message name alone
Set a correlation key expression like =orderId on the boundary error event attached to the message catch; correlation only fires on boundary events

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8 messages need a name and a correlation key expression on the message reference; both must match the published message at runtime.

c.text: Incorrect. The message name alone identifies the subscription, not the specific instance; a correlation key value is also required.

d.text: Incorrect. Boundary error events match error codes, not message correlation keys; correlation is a property of message references.

a.text: Incorrect. Tenant id is an isolation mechanism, not a per-instance correlation key; the documented control is the correlation key expression.

explanation: Camunda 8 message correlation requires a message name plus a FEEL correlation key expression on the message reference; the broker matches the published message to the unique waiting instance.

scenario: Orion Insurance evaluates 5000 claims per day in Camunda 8.8 through a parallel multi-instance review task with 7 reviewers per claim. The team must end the multi-instance activity as soon as 3 reviewers approve rather than waiting for every reviewer to complete the loop.
question: Which Camunda 8 multi-instance property models this short-circuit early-termination rule?

options.text: Set loop cardinality to 3 on the multi-instance task so the engine creates only 3 reviewer iterations regardless of the original list length
Set outputCollection to a FEEL expression with a maximum size of 3 so the engine ignores iterations that would push it past the limit
Set inputCollection to the first 3 elements of the reviewer list using FEEL so the multi-instance only iterates 3 times in total
Set completionCondition to a FEEL expression like = count(approvers) >= 3 so the multi-instance ends once 3 iterations approve

d.text: Correct. The completionCondition is a FEEL boolean expression that, when true, ends the multi-instance activity early and cancels the remaining iterations.

c.text: Incorrect. Trimming the input list pre-selects reviewers; it does not implement a dynamic 3-of-7 short-circuit at runtime.

a.text: Incorrect. Loop cardinality fixes the count up front and cannot wait for 3 approvals among 7 created iterations.

b.text: Incorrect. outputCollection size is not a stop condition; the completionCondition expression is the documented short-circuit control.

explanation: The completionCondition on a multi-instance marker is a FEEL boolean expression evaluated after each iteration; when it becomes true, the engine ends the activity and cancels remaining iterations.

scenario: North Marketplace processes 14000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. A multi-instance service task must run once per element of a list called lineItems on the process scope. The team must configure exactly which collection iterations consume and which variable each iteration sees.
question: Which two configuration fields on the multi-instance task are correct?

options.text: Set inputCollection to a FEEL expression evaluating to the list, and set inputElement to the variable name each iteration should see
Set the loop cardinality to a FEEL expression returning the list, and set inputElement to the variable name each iteration should see
Set outputCollection to a FEEL expression evaluating to the list, and set outputElement to the variable name each iteration should see
Set the inputCollection on the parent process scope, not on the activity, and use a per-element FEEL header to expose the element to each iteration

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8 multi-instance markers use inputCollection for the iteration list and inputElement for the per-iteration variable name.

c.text: Incorrect. outputCollection accumulates per-iteration outputs into a list; it does not drive the iteration count.

b.text: Incorrect. Loop cardinality is an integer count; inputCollection is the list-driven mechanism that pairs with inputElement.

d.text: Incorrect. The multi-instance configuration lives on the activity element itself, not on the parent process scope via a header.

explanation: Camunda 8 multi-instance markers use inputCollection (the list) and inputElement (the per-iteration variable name) to drive list-based iteration.

scenario: Zenith Insurance processes 6000 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. A parallel multi-instance service task computes a per-iteration variable priceEur; on completion the team must collect every iteration's priceEur into a single list called prices on the parent scope for downstream FEEL aggregation.
question: Which two properties configure the per-iteration output collection on the multi-instance task?

options.text: Set outputCollection to priceEur and outputElement to prices on the multi-instance marker so the engine reverses the mapping at completion
Set inputCollection to prices and inputElement to priceEur on the multi-instance marker so each iteration contributes its priceEur to the list
Set outputCollection to prices and outputElement to priceEur on the multi-instance marker so each iteration contributes its priceEur to the list
Set loop cardinality to a FEEL expression like count(prices) and let the engine accumulate priceEur into prices automatically at completion

c.text: Correct. outputCollection names the target list on the parent scope, and outputElement names the per-iteration variable that contributes to the list.

b.text: Incorrect. inputCollection and inputElement drive the iteration input, not the collected output list on the parent scope.

a.text: Incorrect. The roles are not reversible: outputCollection is the list name, outputElement is the per-iteration value name.

d.text: Incorrect. Loop cardinality is just a count; it does not collect per-iteration variables into an output list.

explanation: outputCollection names the list on the parent scope and outputElement names the per-iteration variable that contributes to that list at completion.

scenario: Beacon Marketplace runs 8000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. A service task's job worker completes with a variable result, but downstream activities expect the value as priceEur on the process scope. The team must transform that one variable without writing FEEL inside every downstream activity.
question: Which Camunda 8 task configuration maps the job's result variable into priceEur on the process scope?

options.text: Configure a custom header source=result and target=priceEur on the service task; the engine performs the rename on completion of the task
Add an input mapping with source result and target priceEur on the service task; the engine writes priceEur into the parent scope on completion
Add an output mapping with source result and target priceEur on the service task; the engine writes priceEur into the parent scope on completion
Define a process script task with FEEL between this task and every downstream consumer to evaluate priceEur on the parent scope each time

c.text: Correct. Output mappings run on task completion: the source FEEL expression evaluates against the task scope and writes the result under target on the parent scope.

b.text: Incorrect. Input mappings run at activation, not on completion; renaming the job's output is the documented role of output mappings.

d.text: Incorrect. Output mappings on a single task are simpler and avoid sprinkling FEEL across every downstream activity.

a.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static metadata for the worker; they do not transform variables on task completion.

explanation: Output mappings evaluate FEEL on task completion against the task scope and propagate the result to the parent scope under the configured target name.

scenario: Sable Insurance starts 10000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 through the create-process-instance API. Each instance must initialise the process variables retryCount=0 and currencyCode=EUR at the start event without relying on any downstream input mapping or service task call.
question: Which Camunda 8 mechanism seeds variables on a new process instance at start time?

options.text: Pass the variables in the create-process-instance API payload, or set an input mapping on the start event of the process to compute them
Declare initial variables in the BPMN file under a process-level documentation element so the engine reads them at every new instance start
Add a service task before the start event so the engine activates a worker that writes the initial values to the process scope before the actual start
Define the variables in application.properties on the broker so every new process instance receives them at start across all process definitions

a.text: Correct. New instances accept a variables payload on the create-process-instance API, and start events also support input mappings to initialise variables.

b.text: Incorrect. Documentation is human-readable metadata and is not consulted by the engine for variable initialisation.

d.text: Incorrect. Broker config does not seed process variables; initialisation is at the process or instance level, not the broker level.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no element before the start event in BPMN; the start event itself is the entry point of the process.

explanation: Camunda 8 initialises variables at start either via the create-process-instance API payload or via input mappings on the start event of the process definition.

scenario: Keystone Telco broadcasts a tenant-scoped signal across 6 microservices in Camunda 8.8 with 8000 events per day. The signal name must include the tenant id at throw time (e.g., "market-down-" + tenantId) so only the tenant-specific subscribers react to the broadcast.
question: How is the dynamic signal name configured on a throw signal event in Camunda 8?

options.text: Set the signal name expression on the throw event to a FEEL expression like ="market-down-" + tenantId; the engine resolves it at throw time
Set the signal name on the throw event to a literal market-down and add a correlation key tenantId; Camunda 8 signals support correlation keys
Set the signal name on the throw event to a JUEL expression referencing tenantId; the engine reads tenant id as a per-instance JUEL variable for this case
Set the signal name to the literal market-down on the throw event; tenant scoping is added by the engine from the runtime tenant id implicitly

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8 signal events accept a FEEL name expression evaluated at throw time, which produces the runtime signal name for subscribers.

d.text: Incorrect. The engine does not implicitly prefix signal names with tenant ids; that scoping must come from the expression.

c.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 uses FEEL, not JUEL, for runtime expressions on BPMN signal name fields.

b.text: Incorrect. Signals are broadcast and have no correlation key like messages do; scoping is achieved by encoding the value in the name expression.

explanation: Camunda 8 signal events accept a FEEL name expression evaluated at throw time; subscribers receive the broadcast by exact name match.

scenario: Helios Payroll triggers a closing process for 5000 records per day in Camunda 8.8. A timer start event must fire 3 times in total at exactly 24 hours between each firing and stop on the third occurrence rather than continuing indefinitely.
question: Which cycle expression on the timer start event matches this finite repeating schedule?

options.text: PT72H is an ISO 8601 duration expression that fires 3 times at 24 hour intervals on a timer start event in Camunda 8
R3/2026-01-01T00:00 is an ISO 8601 date expression that fires 3 times at 24 hour intervals from the listed anchor date
R/PT24H is an ISO 8601 repeating cycle that fires 3 times at 24 hour intervals and then stops on the third firing
R3/PT24H is an ISO 8601 repeating cycle that fires 3 times at 24 hour intervals and then stops on the third firing

d.text: Correct. ISO 8601 cycles use R<count>/<duration>: R3/PT24H fires 3 times at 24 hour intervals and then stops.

c.text: Incorrect. R/PT24H with no count is unbounded and would not stop on the third firing.

a.text: Incorrect. PT72H is a duration of 72 hours, not a repeating cycle that fires at 24 hour intervals.

b.text: Incorrect. The /-separated date form is not how cycle counts are encoded; a duration follows the R<count>/ prefix.

explanation: ISO 8601 cycle expressions use R<count>/<duration>; R3/PT24H is the documented form for a finite repeat of 3 firings at 24 hour intervals.

scenario: Granite Insurance must hold 4500 claims per day for 30 minutes before re-checking an external system in Camunda 8.8. The team configures an intermediate timer catch event that waits exactly 30 minutes from token arrival and then continues the process flow.
question: Which timer expression format on the intermediate timer catch event is correct for the 30 minutes wait?

options.text: A FEEL number expression like 30 evaluates to a 30 minutes wait when configured as the timer duration on the intermediate event
A FEEL string expression like 30m evaluates to a 30 minutes wait when configured as the timer duration on the intermediate event
An ISO 8601 cycle expression like R1/PT30M evaluates to a one-shot 30 minutes wait when configured as the timer cycle on the intermediate event
An ISO 8601 duration expression like PT30M evaluates to a 30 minutes wait from the moment the token arrives at the timer event

d.text: Correct. Camunda 8 timer durations use ISO 8601 duration strings (e.g., PT30M for 30 minutes) on intermediate timer events.

a.text: Incorrect. A bare number is not a valid timer duration; the engine expects an ISO 8601 duration string.

b.text: Incorrect. 30m is not the ISO 8601 syntax; the engine requires the PT-prefixed form like PT30M.

c.text: Incorrect. Cycle expressions are used for repeating starts, not one-shot intermediate waits; the duration form is canonical here.

explanation: Camunda 8 timer events use ISO 8601 duration strings (PT30M for 30 minutes) for intermediate one-shot waits.

scenario: Pinnacle Bank handles 9500 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A user task must be assigned to the variable accountManager at activation rather than to a static user. The team must configure the user task so Tasklist shows the task to the dynamic assignee resolved at runtime.
question: How does the modeler assign a user task to a value resolved from a process variable at activation?

options.text: Set the assignee property to the literal accountManager so the engine treats the value as a variable reference for backward compatibility
Set the candidate users property to a FEEL expression like =accountManager so Tasklist treats candidate users as the actual assignee at activation
Set the assignee property to a JUEL expression referencing accountManager so Camunda 8 evaluates JUEL expressions to resolve the runtime user identifier
Set the assignee property to a FEEL expression like =accountManager so the engine resolves the value at activation and writes it to the task

d.text: Correct. The assignee property on a user task accepts a FEEL expression in Camunda 8; the engine resolves it at activation to a user identifier.

a.text: Incorrect. The literal value is treated as a user name string and is not implicitly resolved against a variable.

b.text: Incorrect. Candidate users define visibility, not the actual assignee; the assignee field is the documented assignment control.

c.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 uses FEEL, not JUEL, for runtime expressions on user task properties.

explanation: User tasks accept a FEEL assignee expression in Camunda 8; the engine evaluates it at activation and writes the resolved user identifier to the task.

scenario: Quantum Marketplace runs 2000 events per day in Camunda 8.8 across 3 candidate groups in Tasklist. A user task must be visible to a group selected dynamically per process instance based on the variable productLine. The team must configure the user task accordingly.
question: Which user task property selects the candidate group from a process variable at activation?

options.text: Set the candidate users property to a FEEL expression like =productLine and let Tasklist expand the value into the matching candidate group
Set the candidate groups property to a FEEL expression like =productLine so the engine resolves the group at activation and exposes the task
Set the assignee property to a FEEL expression like =productLine and let Tasklist convert the user name to a group automatically at sign-in
Set the candidate groups property to the literal productLine so the engine treats the value as a variable reference for backward compatibility

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8 user tasks accept a FEEL candidate groups expression evaluated at activation; the engine writes the resolved group id to the task.

d.text: Incorrect. The literal value is a group name string, not an implicit variable reference; explicit FEEL expressions are required.

c.text: Incorrect. Assignee writes the owner; it does not convert a user name into a candidate group implicitly in Tasklist.

a.text: Incorrect. Candidate users define a per-user allow list; they do not expand into group ids in Camunda 8 Tasklist.

explanation: Candidate groups on a user task accept a FEEL expression in Camunda 8; the engine evaluates it at activation to determine which Tasklist groups see the task.

scenario: Catalyst Logistics handles 6500 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. A subprocess uses a temporary variable named iter inside a multi-instance loop. The team must keep iter visible only inside the subprocess scope and never expose it on the parent process variables after the subprocess completes.
question: Which Camunda 8 mechanism keeps a variable scoped to its subprocess and prevents propagation to the parent scope?

options.text: Set the variable on the subprocess scope directly (e.g., via an input mapping on the subprocess) so iter is local to that scope
Define the variable on the root process scope and add an output mapping on every parent activity that filters iter out of the propagation
Annotate the variable as private in the BPMN extension element; the engine honours private variables and never propagates them to parents
Define the variable as a custom header on the subprocess element; custom headers are scoped to the element and never become process variables

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8 variables are created on the scope where they are set; a variable defined on the subprocess scope stays local to that scope.

b.text: Incorrect. Root-scope variables propagate to descendants; filtering on every parent activity is fragile compared with scoping at creation.

c.text: Incorrect. Camunda BPMN has no private variable annotation; visibility is determined by the scope where the variable is created.

d.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static metadata, not process variables; they do not provide a runtime variable scoping mechanism.

explanation: Variables created on a subprocess scope are local to that scope; propagation to the parent scope happens only when explicitly mapped via output mappings.

scenario: Pinnacle Retail, a marketplace handling 90000 orders per day, calls a tax-rate REST API from Camunda 8.8. When the API returns HTTP 404, the order is a business case that must route to a manual-review user task via a boundary error event; when it returns HTTP 504, the call should fail the job with 2 retries and a 30 second backoff.
question: Which Error Expression correctly distinguishes these two outcomes?

options.text: Throw a ConnectorException with code 404 and a different ConnectorException with code 504 directly from the FEEL Error Expression in the modeler properties panel
Return bpmnError("504", "timeout") for code 504 and ignoreError() for code 404 so the workflow continues with no boundary handling needed
Return bpmnError("404", "not found") for code 404 and jobError("gateway timeout", {}, job.retries - 1, @"PT30S") for code 504 from a single FEEL expression
Use jobError for both codes and rely on the boundary event configuration on the BPMN element to differentiate the 2 outcomes at runtime

c.text: Correct. The bpmnError function triggers a BPMN error event for business outcomes, and jobError fails the job with custom retries and an ISO 8601 retry backoff for transient technical failures.

b.text: Incorrect. The 404 case is the business outcome that must route to manual review, so it requires bpmnError, not ignoreError, which would complete the job silently.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL Error Expressions cannot throw Java exceptions directly; they return BPMN error or job error contexts via the provided helper functions.

d.text: Incorrect. jobError for both eliminates the boundary-event routing entirely and merges a business outcome with a transient technical failure.

explanation: Error Expression uses bpmnError to raise BPMN errors for business outcomes that boundary events should catch, and jobError to fail the job with optional retries and an ISO 8601 retry backoff for transient failures.

docs.title: How to use connectors - BPMN errors and failing jobs

scenario: Beacon Energy, a utility provider with 400000 customers, configures a Camunda 8.8 REST connector to call a billing API requiring 3 HTTP headers: Authorization (Bearer token), X-Tenant-Id (process variable tenantId), and Accept (literal application/json) for every customer-balance query.
question: Which HTTP Headers field value is the documented format?

options.text: Authorization: Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}; X-Tenant-Id: tenantId; Accept: application/json as a semicolon-separated plain-text header list
= { headers: [ { name: "Authorization", value: "Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}" } ] } as a nested context with a headers list inside
[ "Authorization=Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}", "X-Tenant-Id=tenantId", "Accept=application/json" ] as a FEEL list of key-value strings
= { Authorization: "Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}", "X-Tenant-Id": tenantId, Accept: "application/json" } as a FEEL context map

d.text: Correct. HTTP Headers in the REST connector accept a FEEL context (map) where keys are header names and values are FEEL expressions or quoted strings including secrets.

a.text: Incorrect. The field expects a FEEL context value, not a semicolon-separated plain-text string, and secrets must be quoted inside FEEL.

c.text: Incorrect. The format is a map, not a list of equal-delimited strings; the connector does not parse that shape into header pairs.

b.text: Incorrect. The connector reads the headers from the field's direct map; there is no nested headers list inside another context object.

explanation: The REST connector Query parameters and HTTP Headers fields are configured using the FEEL context (map) data type. Each entry maps a header name to a FEEL expression or quoted string.

scenario: Helios Logistics calls a weather API from Camunda 8.8 across 5000 requests per hour. The REST connector returns a large JSON document, and the team only needs 3 fields (temperature, windspeed, humidity) mapped into separate process variables that downstream FEEL gateways can compare in 30 seconds or less.
question: Which connector output mapping should the team use for this requirement?

options.text: Use Result Variable named response and write a downstream service task that re-reads the full JSON document and extracts the 3 fields after the connector completes
Use both Result Variable and Result Expression on the same task to write the full response and the 3 named variables, doubling the FEEL evaluation cost per call
Use Result Expression with a FEEL context like = { temp: response.body.main.temp, wind: response.body.wind.speed } to project specific fields into named process variables
Use Result Variable with a comma-separated list of field paths such as response.body.main.temp,response.body.wind.speed so the runtime extracts each one

c.text: Correct. Result Expression is a FEEL context expression that maps fields from the response into multiple named process variables, which is the documented approach for projection.

a.text: Incorrect. Storing the whole response and re-parsing it in a downstream task adds an unnecessary step when Result Expression projects directly during the same task.

d.text: Incorrect. Result Variable accepts a single variable name only; field projection is the job of Result Expression with a FEEL context.

b.text: Incorrect. The two fields are not designed to be combined as a pair on the same task for this use case; Result Expression alone covers the projection requirement.

explanation: Result Variable stores the full response under one variable name. Result Expression is a FEEL context expression that projects selected fields from the response into multiple named process variables.

docs.title: How to use connectors - variable and response mapping

scenario: Meridian Insurance is building a custom outbound connector with the Camunda 8.8 Connector SDK to score 60000 transactions per day against a proprietary fraud API. The Java class annotated @OutboundConnector exposes the name MyFraudConnector with type io.camunda:fraud-connector:1 and must be reachable by the connector runtime as a job worker within 5 seconds of deployment.
question: Which mechanism connects the BPMN task to this Java implementation at runtime?

options.text: The element template declares a hidden property bound to zeebe:taskDefinition with the property type set to the same string io.camunda:fraud-connector:1 so the job type matches
The connector runtime scans the BPMN XML for the literal class name MyFraudConnector and instantiates it directly through Java reflection at deploy time
The element template stores the fully qualified Java class name in a zeebe:taskHeader so the runtime can load it from the classpath when the job activates
The connector runtime registers a single global job type for every custom connector and dispatches by inspecting the input variables on each activated job

a.text: Correct. The element template binds zeebe:taskDefinition.type to the value declared in @OutboundConnector, which is the job type the connector worker fetches.

b.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not scan the BPMN XML for Java class names; the binding is via the job type registered in zeebe:taskDefinition.

c.text: Incorrect. Job headers carry per-task configuration such as resultVariable or errorExpression, not the connector implementation class to load.

d.text: Incorrect. Each custom connector registers its own job type; there is no single global type that dispatches by input shape across all connectors.

explanation: An outbound connector's @OutboundConnector type matches the zeebe:taskDefinition type value declared in the element template. The connector runtime registers a job worker for that exact type to fetch and execute jobs.

docs.title: Connector SDK - outbound connector element template

scenario: Aurora Bank, a fintech processing $4M of card transactions per hour, configures a REST connector in Camunda 8.8 with an Authorization header carrying a bearer token. The team must avoid exposing the API key in the BPMN XML and references a cluster secret named REST_API_KEY from a FEEL header map across 12 connector tasks.
question: Which expression correctly references the secret inside a FEEL header map?

options.text: Use double curly braces inside a quoted string such as = { Authorization: "Bearer {{secrets.REST_API_KEY}}" } so the placeholder is resolved at runtime
Inject the secret value into the BPMN XML at deploy time using a build-time placeholder so the connector receives the literal string at runtime
Reference the secret directly without quotes such as = { Authorization: secrets.REST_API_KEY } so FEEL treats it as a variable lookup
Configure the secret as a process variable on the start event and pass it through input mappings to each connector task on the model

a.text: Correct. Secrets use the {{secrets.NAME}} placeholder and must be wrapped in double quotes when used inside a FEEL expression such as a header map.

c.text: Incorrect. The bare secrets.* syntax is deprecated; the current placeholder is {{secrets.NAME}} wrapped in double quotes inside FEEL.

b.text: Incorrect. Secrets are resolved by the connector runtime from the cluster secret store, not baked into the BPMN XML at deploy time.

d.text: Incorrect. Connector secrets are managed at the cluster level and are not exposed as process variables to other tasks.

explanation: Secrets are referenced with the placeholder {{secrets.NAME}}. Inside FEEL expressions they must be wrapped in double quotes so the connector runtime can replace the placeholder with the actual secret value.

scenario: Stellar Telco, a carrier processing 250000 SIM activations per day, uses a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook connector on a message intermediate catch event. The vendor POSTs {"orderId":"A-123","status":"paid"} and the engine must correlate the message to the running process instance whose orderId process variable equals A-123 within 5 seconds.
question: Which pair of correlation key settings is correct?

options.text: Correlation key (process) = orderId and Correlation key (payload) = request.headers.orderId so the engine reads the value from the HTTP request headers section
Correlation key (process) = request.params.orderId and Correlation key (payload) = orderId so the URL parameter is matched against the process variable name
Correlation key (process) = orderId and Correlation key (payload) = request.body.orderId so both sides resolve to A-123 for correlation
Correlation key (process) = request.body.orderId and Correlation key (payload) = orderId so the variable lookup happens against the incoming HTTP body

c.text: Correct. Correlation key (process) names the process variable that subscriptions use; Correlation key (payload) is a FEEL expression that extracts the same value from the incoming request.

d.text: Incorrect. The two fields are reversed; process side references the process variable, payload side reads from the request object.

a.text: Incorrect. The incoming body field is request.body.orderId; the headers form is only correct when the vendor places the correlation value in an HTTP header.

b.text: Incorrect. URL parameters live under request.params and the process side must be a variable name, not a request expression.

explanation: Correlation key (process) is a FEEL expression referring to the process variable that is used as the subscription key. Correlation key (payload) is a FEEL expression that extracts the matching value from the incoming webhook request.

scenario: Catalyst SaaS, a publisher handling 80000 events per day, integrates Slack with a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook connector. Slack performs a one-time verification challenge by POSTing {"type":"url_verification","challenge":"abc"} and expects the body {"challenge":"abc"} returned with HTTP 200 in under 3 seconds; otherwise normal process correlation must run.
question: Which Verification expression handles the Slack challenge without creating a process instance?

options.text: = if request.body.type = "url_verification" then { challenge: request.body.challenge } else null so the connector returns the raw body and still triggers a process instance for each call
= if request.body.type = "url_verification" then { body: { challenge: request.body.challenge }, statusCode: 200 } else null so the webhook returns the challenge and skips correlation
Set a Response Expression instead of a Verification Expression so the webhook continues normal correlation while also returning the challenge body to Slack
Configure an Activation Condition of = request.body.type != "url_verification" so verification calls are silently dropped without any HTTP response to Slack

b.text: Correct. When the verification expression returns a non-null value, the webhook returns that response immediately and skips activation, HMAC, and correlation.

a.text: Incorrect. Without wrapping in a body object and an explicit statusCode the response format is incomplete, and correlation would still run on the verification call.

d.text: Incorrect. Activation Condition drops the call from process correlation but does not return a custom HTTP response to the caller, which Slack requires.

c.text: Incorrect. Response Expression runs after correlation completes; the verification step must run before correlation is attempted at all.

explanation: The Verification Expression returns a response object (body, statusCode, headers) and short-circuits the entire webhook pipeline so no process instance is created. Returning null falls through to normal processing.

scenario: Delta Bank reviews 8000 requests per day in a Camunda 8.8 leave-application process. The DMN table refuses applications when the applicant has no vacation days left or is currently in probation. Both rules may match for one applicant, but the team wants the table to return a single refuse output without raising an error.
question: Which hit policy is the correct choice for this rule overlap pattern, given the constraints?

options.text: UNIQUE hit policy returns one output and raises an evaluation incident whenever any two rules match the same applicant input
ANY hit policy returns one output when multiple rules match, on the condition that every satisfied rule produces the same output
FIRST hit policy returns the first matched rule's output and does not depend on the equality of overlapping outputs
COLLECT hit policy returns one output by definition whenever multiple satisfied rules produce the same refuse value

b.text: Correct. The DMN docs describe ANY as allowing multiple rules to match if and only if they generate the same output, which the refuse use case satisfies.

c.text: Incorrect. FIRST works regardless of output equality, but the question explicitly relies on the equal-output guarantee of ANY.

a.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE raises an incident on any second match; the scenario explicitly expects overlapping rules without an error.

d.text: Incorrect. COLLECT returns a list (or aggregated value) and never a single bare output without an aggregator definition.

explanation: ANY allows multiple satisfied rules only when their outputs are identical and returns that common output once, matching the refuse decision scenario.

scenario: Keystone Insurance evaluates 16000 transactions per day through a BPMN business rule task in Camunda 8.8. The DMN file is deployed alongside the process and contains the decision with ID quoteDecision and name Quote Decision. The team needs the task to invoke that exact decision and write its result to a process variable.
question: How is the business rule task configured to invoke the deployed DMN decision and write its result back into the process?

options.text: Set the task type to dmn and let the engine match the decision by the process file name written next to the business rule task
Implement a Java delegate annotated @DecisionEvaluator on the business rule task to load the DMN file from the classpath at runtime
Set the decision name property to Quote Decision and the result variable to quoteResult on the business rule task
Set the decision ID property to quoteDecision and the result variable property to a name like quoteResult on the business rule task

d.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 business rule task properties panel binds the decision by its decision ID and writes the result under the configured result variable.

c.text: Incorrect. The decision is referenced by its technical ID, not by its human-readable display name.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no implicit binding by file name; the business rule task must reference the decision explicitly by ID.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 does not use Java delegates on business rule tasks; the DMN engine evaluates the table natively after deployment.

explanation: Camunda 8 business rule tasks call DMN decisions by decision ID and store the evaluation output under the configured result variable on the task.

scenario: Catalyst Logistics built a DMN table in Camunda 8.8 that picks delivery promotions for an order of 250 shipments per day. Several rules may match a single visitor, and the team wants every matching promotion code returned to the process to display them all in an Operate-visible variable.
question: Which hit policy returns every satisfied rule's output as a list with no defined ordering and no aggregation?

options.text: COLLECT hit policy with no aggregator returns the outputs of all satisfied rules as a list in arbitrary order
UNIQUE hit policy returns a list of one item when multiple rules match the same input data combination
RULE ORDER hit policy returns the outputs as a list but only if you explicitly set an aggregation attribute on the decision table
ANY hit policy returns a list of every satisfied output, regardless of whether the outputs are equal to each other

a.text: Correct. The DMN docs describe COLLECT without an aggregator as returning a list of outputs in arbitrary order.

c.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER already orders its list by rule position and never requires an aggregation attribute to do so.

d.text: Incorrect. ANY violates the policy when satisfied rules have different outputs; it does not return a list.

b.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE returns one output entry and raises an error if two or more rules match.

explanation: COLLECT without an aggregator returns the outputs of all satisfied rules as a list in arbitrary order, which is the documented behaviour for the case of collecting matched promotions.

scenario: Beacon Payroll computes annual salary bonuses for 4000 employees through a Camunda 8.8 business rule task. The DMN table has one numeric output column called bonusEur. Tenure rules overlap so an employee with 3.5 years of service should match three rules whose individual amounts must be summed into a single value such as 600.
question: Which hit policy and aggregator combination yields a single summed output entry from the satisfied rules?

options.text: RULE ORDER hit policy with the SUM aggregator returns one output value equal to the sum of all satisfied rules' output entries
COLLECT hit policy with no aggregator returns one output value equal to the sum of all satisfied rules' output entries
COLLECT hit policy with the SUM aggregator returns one output value equal to the sum of all satisfied rules' output entries
FIRST hit policy with the SUM aggregator returns one output value equal to the sum of all satisfied rules' output entries

c.text: Correct. COLLECT with the SUM aggregator returns a single output that is the sum of every satisfied rule's output, matching the 600 example in the docs.

b.text: Incorrect. COLLECT without an aggregator returns the satisfied rules' outputs as a list, not a single summed value.

a.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER does not accept an aggregator attribute and always returns the list of matched outputs in rule order.

d.text: Incorrect. The aggregation attribute is only valid for COLLECT in Camunda DMN; FIRST cannot be combined with SUM.

explanation: Per the DMN docs, the aggregation attribute is only valid for the COLLECT hit policy; the SUM aggregator returns one output that equals the sum of every satisfied rule's output.

scenario: Helios Insurance ships a new DMN decision table to Camunda 8.8 that classifies 12000 claims per day into risk bands. The modeler did not select a hit policy in the properties panel, and the team wants to confirm what the deployed table will do when more than one rule matches the input data.
question: Which hit policy will the deployed decision table use, and what happens if two rules match the same input?

options.text: UNIQUE is the default hit policy, and a matching pair of rules raises an evaluation error reported as an incident on the business rule task
FIRST is the default hit policy, and the engine quietly returns only the output of the first overlapping rule defined in the table
COLLECT is the default hit policy, and overlapping rule outputs are returned to the process as a list with arbitrary ordering
RULE ORDER is the default hit policy, and overlapping rules emit an ordered list of outputs to the calling business rule task

a.text: Correct. The DMN docs state that when no hit policy is set the default UNIQUE applies; a second matching rule violates UNIQUE and the evaluation fails.

c.text: Incorrect. COLLECT must be set explicitly on the decisionTable XML element and is never the implicit default in Camunda DMN.

b.text: Incorrect. FIRST is a separate explicit policy and is not chosen by Camunda when the hitPolicy attribute is omitted from the decision table.

d.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER must be set explicitly and is not the default behaviour for a decision table without a hit policy attribute.

explanation: The DMN spec implemented by Camunda 8.8 defaults to UNIQUE when no hit policy is set; multiple matches violate UNIQUE and the engine raises an evaluation incident on the business rule task.

scenario: Iris Healthcare evaluates 7500 records per day with a Camunda 8.8 DMN table. The team must classify by age band derived as years between the variable birthDate and today, but the underlying process variable only carries birthDate, not age. The first input column header is responsible for that derivation.
question: How does the modeler compute the age value used by the rules of the input column?

options.text: Define the age value as a hidden output column with a FEEL output entry, then reference that output column as the input column
Embed a Java expression in the column header through the namespace attribute on the decisionTable XML element
Put a FEEL input expression like years and months duration(birthDate, today()).years in the column header used to evaluate each rule
Add a process input mapping with a FEEL script that overwrites the birthDate variable with the computed age value before evaluation

c.text: Correct. Input column headers in Camunda DMN accept a FEEL input expression that is evaluated once per decision call against the input data and process variables.

d.text: Incorrect. Process input mappings work for business rule task input variables, but the documented place for the derivation is the column input expression itself.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda DMN uses FEEL for input expressions in 8.8; Java expressions are not a supported decision-table column language here.

a.text: Incorrect. Output columns produce results, not inputs to rules; you cannot wire an output back into the input header.

explanation: Camunda DMN input column headers carry a FEEL input expression evaluated against the input data; this is the documented way to derive a value such as age from birthDate.

scenario: Aurora Retail uses a DMN table in Camunda 8.8 to pick a single advertisement banner for each visitor. The table contains 6 overlapping age-based rules, and the team wants only the top rule in display order to win when several rules match a 19 year old customer browsing 250 products per session.
question: Which hit policy delivers exactly one output equal to the topmost satisfied rule's output entry?

options.text: ANY hit policy returns one output, but only if all 6 rules that match produce the same exact output entry
UNIQUE hit policy returns at most one output but raises an evaluation error if more than one of the 6 rules matches
RULE ORDER hit policy still returns every satisfied rule, just in the order in which the rules appear in the table
FIRST hit policy returns only the output of the first satisfied rule according to the rule ordering in the decision table

d.text: Correct. FIRST takes only the first satisfied rule's output by rule order, which is exactly what the team wants for the banner choice.

b.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE forbids multiple matches; the scenario explicitly expects overlapping rules and would raise an evaluation error.

c.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER returns a list of all satisfied rules' outputs, not the single first one.

a.text: Incorrect. ANY needs identical outputs across satisfied rules, which different banner choices do not provide.

explanation: FIRST is the documented Camunda DMN hit policy that returns the output of the first satisfied rule by row order, which fits the single-banner selection use case.

scenario: Harbor Telco computes a single derived value for 25000 calls per day in Camunda 8.8: callCostEur = duration * ratePerSecondEur. The team does not want a 1-row decision table and prefers expressing the formula inline without input columns, output columns, or hit policy ceremony.
question: Which decision logic type fits a single FEEL expression that returns one value without input/output columns?

options.text: A knowledge source describes the authority and computes the FEEL expression at deployment time on behalf of the decision
A decision table with one rule and the UNIQUE hit policy is the only supported way to express a single-formula decision
A decision literal expression evaluates a single FEEL expression and returns one value as the decision's result
A business knowledge model defines a reusable function and replaces the decision element entirely in the DMN file

c.text: Correct. The DMN modeler docs list decision literal expression as a supported decision logic type for evaluating a single FEEL expression and returning one value.

b.text: Incorrect. A single-rule table works but is unnecessary; the documented purpose of a literal expression is exactly this case.

d.text: Incorrect. A business knowledge model is invoked from a decision; it does not replace the decision element itself.

a.text: Incorrect. Knowledge sources have no execution semantics and are ignored during evaluation.

explanation: Decision literal expression is the documented decision logic type for evaluating a single FEEL expression and returning one value, with no table ceremony.

scenario: Junction Insurance evaluates 14000 policies per day in Camunda 8.8 for motor cover. One DMN table named pricing has 2 output columns named premiumEur and discountPct and uses the UNIQUE hit policy. The team must read both fields downstream from the business rule task's resultVariable.
question: What is the shape of the decision result returned to the calling business rule task for one matching rule?

options.text: A list of contexts of length 1 is returned because every decision table evaluation produces a list of matched rows, regardless of hit policy
Only the first output column premiumEur is returned because the resultVariable is always scalar for tables with the UNIQUE hit policy
Two separate top-level variables premiumEur and discountPct are returned, replacing the resultVariable with two fields on the parent scope
A single context such as { premiumEur: 480, discountPct: 5 } is returned because UNIQUE produces one matched row with two named outputs

d.text: Correct. For UNIQUE with multiple output columns, the result is a context (FEEL map) of the named outputs of the single matched rule.

c.text: Incorrect. The business rule task writes one resultVariable; named outputs are nested within that variable, not flattened onto the parent scope automatically.

a.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE returns a single matched rule's output as a context, not a list; lists arise from RULE ORDER or COLLECT (without aggregator).

b.text: Incorrect. Every named output column appears in the result of a matched rule; the engine does not silently drop additional output columns.

explanation: A UNIQUE table with two output columns returns a single context (FEEL map) containing both named outputs for the matched rule, exposed via the business rule task's resultVariable.

scenario: Forge Catering models a DRG in Camunda 8.8 for 1200 events per year across its dinner-service contracts. The decision beverages depends on the result of the decision dish, which is defined in the same DMN file. The team needs the beverages table to consume the dish output without writing a process variable in between.
question: How is the dependency between the beverages decision and the dish decision modeled and resolved in DMN?

options.text: An authorityRequirement element with a requiredDecision href of #dish makes the dish output available inside beverages by the decision name Dish
An informationRequirement element with a requiredInput href of #dish makes the dish output available inside beverages by the input data ID dish
An informationRequirement element with a requiredDecision href of #dish makes the dish output available inside beverages by the decision ID dish

c.text: Correct. The DRG docs show requiredDecision inside informationRequirement with href=#decisionId; the result is then accessed by that decision ID in the dependent decision.

b.text: Incorrect. requiredInput points to an inputData element, not to another decision; it cannot reference the dish decision.

explanation: DRG dependencies are declared with informationRequirement/requiredDecision pointing to the decision ID by href; the dependent decision references the result by that ID at evaluation.

scenario: Granite Bank wires a DRG in Camunda 8.8 across 9000 loan applications per day. The required decision creditScore has 2 output columns named band and score and uses the UNIQUE hit policy. The dependent decision underwriting must read both outputs from the upstream result during evaluation.

Access the outputs only via process variables that the calling business rule task must explicitly map after the upstream decision runs

b.text: Correct. The DRG docs state that when a required decision has multiple outputs, values are grouped under the decision ID and accessed by output name (decisionId.outputName).

a.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE returns a single matching rule, not a list, and the engine does not expose outputs by positional index in the dependent decision.

d.text: Incorrect. DRG dependencies are resolved natively during DMN evaluation; no intermediate process variable mapping is required for required decisions in the same DRG.

c.text: Incorrect. The decision is referenced by its ID and outputs by their names, not by output IDs or the human-readable decision name.

scenario: Echo Marketplace serves 600 advertisements per minute through a Camunda 8.8 DMN table that picks ad slots by visitor age. ANY hit policy returns a list of every satisfied rule's output, sorted ascending by the rule's row index in the table

b.text: Incorrect. COLLECT without an aggregator returns satisfied rule outputs in arbitrary order, not by rule row position.

c.text: Incorrect. FIRST returns a single output entry, not a list, and the priority page needs every matching ad code.

d.text: Incorrect. ANY does not return a list and forbids satisfied rules with different output entries.

explanation: RULE ORDER returns outputs of all satisfied rules as a list ordered by their position in the decision table, fitting the priority-by-row ad selection.

scenario: Lattice Healthcare evaluates 5000 records per day in Camunda 8.8 through a referral business rule task. The DMN table uses the UNIQUE hit policy but a new release contains 2 overlapping rules. a.text: Correct. A UNIQUE violation is an evaluation error in Camunda 8; the business rule task raises an incident that must be resolved before the process continues.

d.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE forbids more than one match; the engine does not silently pick a rule and continue.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented merge semantics for UNIQUE; overlapping matches are an error state, not a merge operation.

b.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE is order-independent and never picks a winner among overlapping rules in Camunda DMN.

explanation: UNIQUE forbids overlapping matches; the decision evaluation fails, and Camunda 8 raises an incident on the business rule task that an operator must resolve.

scenario: Aurora Bank is onboarding 12 new Camunda 8 developers who need a local environment to model BPMN, deploy to a local Orchestration Cluster, and try inbound webhook connectors. scenario: Aurora Marketplace migrates a TypeScript service that consumes 18000 jobs per day from Camunda 8.7 to Camunda 8.8. The team must replace the legacy SDK class name used to construct a client and update calls to the unified surface introduced in 8.8 across the entire codebase.

scenario: Aurora Telco polls an external REST endpoint to drive a Camunda 8.8 process for 1500 events per day. The integration must run an inbound connector that periodically calls the configured URL and publishes a Zeebe message when the response shape matches a configured condition expression.

scenario: Granite Marketplace exposes a webhook to 6000 events per day in Camunda 8.8. d.text: Incorrect. JMS is not the Webhook connector; queue-based subscriptions are different connector types.

scenario: Forge Insurance writes a custom Camunda 8.8 outbound connector to invoke a private REST API for 7000 transactions per day. scenario: Tessera Insurance configures an HTTP REST outbound connector for 5500 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The team must not embed an API key literal in the BPMN file or element template fields; instead the connector runtime must resolve the key from a managed secret at execution time.

scenario: Harbor Bank packages a custom Camunda 8.8 outbound connector used by 12 modelers for 6000 transactions per day. scenario: Catalyst Bank reads nested JSON for 21000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping must read the field address.city from the variable customer (a context). Use if customer != null then customer.address.city else "UNKNOWN" as a FEEL conditional that branches on the null check before path access

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no optional-chaining operator (?.); explicit conditionals or path-navigation are required.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no built-in coalesce function with that signature; the conditional form is documented.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no try/catch syntax; the conditional form is documented.

scenario: Sable Marketplace runs 7000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping must transform the list of contexts lineItems into a list of strings by formatting each element's id and priceEur fields (e.g., "SKU-42: 9.99") for use as a downstream display list on the parent scope.

for li in lineItems return li.id + ": " + string(li.priceEur) iterates the list with a for-comprehension that returns a string per element

[li.id + ": " + string(li.priceEur) | li in lineItems] uses FEEL list comprehension brackets with the pipe-separator syntax over the source list

b.text: Correct. FEEL's iteration is the for-comprehension: for <var> in <list> return <expr>; it produces a list with one element per iteration.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not method-chained; there is no .map method on lists.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not include a top-level map() higher-order helper.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not use the bracket-pipe list comprehension syntax; for-return is the documented form.

scenario: Ridge Bank classifies 15000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 with FEEL. A FEEL output mapping must return the string "HIGH" when the variable amountCents exceeds 1000000 and otherwise return the string "LOW" as a single value used by a downstream decision table.

case when amountCents > 1000000 then "HIGH" else "LOW" end uses the FEEL case keyword form with explicit when and end markers in a block
if amountCents > 1000000 then "HIGH" else "LOW" uses the FEEL conditional expression keyword form with the documented syntax

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not include the C-style ternary operator.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not include a case-when-then-end statement form.
